MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
Off Calendar Aug-22-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Allen Ruby, Esq. of Skadden Arps Slate Meagher & Flom LLP, T:650.410.4500, for defendant/ moving party Jennifer Johnson;
Robert Moore, Esq. of Allen Matkins Leck Gamble Mallory & Natsis LLP, T:415.273.7465, for plaintiff/opposing party Harold H. Robinson IV;
Joel Zeldin, Esq. of Shartsis Friese LLP, T:415.421.6500, for Silverbelt defendants.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT JENNIFER JOHNSON'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Plaintiff filed an amended complaint after defendants filed these motions. The court would normally take these motions off calendar as moot because the motions attack a superseded pleading. (See State Compensation Insurance Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 CalApp.4th 1124, 1131.) Under the particular circumstances of this case, however, the court will address the merits of these motions because defendants renewed their motions after the amended complaint was filed and plaintiff addresses the merits. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 ["It is well settled that the appearance of a party at the hearing of a motion and his or her opposition to the motion on its merits is a waiver of any defects or irregularities in the notice of motion. This rule applies even when no notice was given at all."].)

Defendant Johnson's motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action. Defendant Johnson fails to maintain her burden of production. The only issue that the separate statement attempts to establish is that the parties did not have a "partnership." (UMF 1-3.) Defendant fails to show that she did not breach her fiduciary duty as a manager member of the LLCs. "The manager of an LLC has a fiduciary duty and owes to the members of the LLC the same duties of loyalty and good faith as a partner owes to the partnership and its partners." (Feresi v. The Livery, LLC (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 419, 425; see also Corps. Code 17704.09(d), (f)(2).)

The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the issue of lost profits. The only claim for damages that a court can summarily adjudicate unless the ruling disposes of an entire cause of action is a claim for punitive damages. (See Code Civ. Proc., 437c(f)(1); DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Superior Court (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 421-422.) Therefore, this aspect of the motion is procedurally infirm, as defendants' reply concedes.

The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the claim for unjust enrichment. A quasi contract claim does not lie where the parties have an express contract that governs their relationship. (See California Medical Association, Inc. v. Aetna US Healthcare of California, Inc. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 151, 172.) Plaintiff is correct that he can plead quantum meruit and breach of contract as alternative theories of relief. (See Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2016) 6 Cal.App.5th1207, 1223.) In this case, however, defendants concede that the contracts are enforceable. (Reply, 8:19-27 ["Defendants have never contested the validity or enforceability of those contracts."].) Plaintiff thus has no further basis to pursue the claim.

Order signed in open court. Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Plaintiff filed an amended complaint after defendants filed these motions. The court would normally take these motions off calendar as moot because the motions attack a superseded pleading. This rule applies even when no notice was given at all."].)

Defendants Silverbelt Holdings, LLC and Silverbelt Investments, LLC's motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action. Corporations Code 17704.09 explains that a member owes a fiduciary duty to another member. It does not provide that an entity LLC such as movants owes such a duty to its members. In fact, plaintiff's amended complaint merely alleges that defendant Johnson breached her fiduciary duty. (First Amended Complaint, Paras. 100-101.)

The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the issue of lost profits. The only claim for damages that a court can summarily adjudicate unless the ruling disposes of an entire cause of action is a claim for punitive damages. (See Code Civ. Proc., 437c(f)(1); DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Superior Court (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 421-422.) Therefore, this aspect of the motion is procedurally infirm, as defendants' reply concedes.

The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the claim for unjust enrichment. A quasi contract claim does not lie where the parties have an express contract that governs their relationship. (See California Medical Association, Inc. v. Aetna US Healthcare of California, Inc. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 151, 172.) Plaintiff is correct that he can plead quantum meruit and breach of contract as alternative theories of relief. (See Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2016) 6 Cal.App.5th 1207, 1223.) In this case, however, defendants concede that the contracts are enforceable. (Reply, 8:19-27 ["Defendants have never contested the validity or enforceability of those contracts."].) Plaintiff thus has no further basis to pursue the claim.

MOTION - Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order
Off Calendar Feb-27-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Grayson Marshall, Esq., Allen Matkins, Telephone 415-273-7442 appearing for Plaintiff;
Joel Seldin, Esq., Shartsis Friese LLP, Telephone 415-4216500, appearing for Defendant(s) Silverbelt Investments, LLC & Silverbelt Holdings, LLC;
Allen Ruby, Esq., Skadde, Telephone 650-470-4500, appearing for Defendant Jennifer Johnson;

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SILVERBELT INVESTMENTS, LLC, SILVERBELT HOLDINGS, LLC Motion For Protective Order

The motion is denied. CCP Section 2025.010 allows Robinson to take the deposition of any person, which is defined to include an entity. Thus, Robinson is entitled to take the deposition of SBH, which is both a "person" and a party to this action. Section 2025.610, on which SBH relies to support its motion, specifically permits a party to take the deposition of a natural person after that person has testified as a PMQ of an entity. Accordingly, it does not support SBH's position. The "good cause" requirement that SBH invokes only applies where a party seeks to take the deposition of a natural person who has previously been deposed individually; it does not apply where a natural person is deposed after testifying as a PMQ. Moreover, SBH's invocation of the Rifkind case is unavailing, as that case only prohibits deposition questions about legal contentions and specifically permits deposition questions about the basis for factual assertions. See Rifkind v. Superior Court, 22 Cal. App. 4th 1255, 1259 (1994). A number of the categories in the deposition notice concern factual assertions rather than legal contentions, and SBH implicitly concedes this, as it agreed to produce a witness in response to many of the categories in the notice. This ruling is without prejudice to a later motion in the event that Robinson seeks to depose Jennifer Johnson or Cyrus Johnson as individuals (though this is not intended to suggest any inclination to grant or deny such a motion, should it ever be filed). Judge Pro Tem Tony Schoenberg will prepare order.

Judge Pro Tem: Tony Schoenberg; Clerk: William Trupek; Not reported. (302/JPT)
